confidence.disclosure: Hallaj has been the subject of one of the great works of modern scholarship — Louis Massignon's four-volume La Passion de Husayn ibn Mansour Hallaj — which reconstructed his itinerary in obsessive detail from the early sources (his son Hamd's eyewitness account of the prison years, the historian al-Tabari, and the Sufi hagiographers). Birth around 858 in Bayda in southern Iran, the years with Sahl al-Tustari from 873, the marriage and apprenticeship at Basra, the three pilgrimages to Mecca, the missionary journeys to India, Turkestan and Khurasan, the imprisonment in Baghdad from 911, and the execution on 26 March 922 are all well attested. The geographic spread is extreme — Hallaj is one of very few early Sufis to have actually reached India and probably the borders of China — but the dating of his missionary years between 894 and 902 is approximate.

sources: Hamd ibn al-Hallaj (eyewitness account of his father's last days)
Louis Massignon, La Passion de Husayn ibn Mansour Hallaj (1922; revised 1975)

journey.event: Born around 858 (244 AH) near al-Bayda in the province of Fars in southern Iran. His father is a cotton-carder (hallaj in Arabic) and an Arabised Iranian; his grandfather is said to have been a Zoroastrian who converted to Islam.

The family migrates through the textile centres of Iran and settles in Wasit, the strict Hanbali town between Baghdad and Basra. Young Hallaj is educated in Arabic grammar and the Qur'an by Hanbali traditionists. By age twelve he has memorised the Qur'an entirely.

At sixteen, leaves the conservative milieu of Wasit and travels to Tustar in Khuzistan to become the disciple of the great Sufi shaykh Sahl al-Tustari, the reclusive mystic and Qur'an commentator. Stays in Sahl's company for two years.

Leaves Tustar at eighteen and travels to Basra, the centre of Iraqi Sufism. Joins the circle of the shaykh Amr al-Makki, who invests him with the patched Sufi robe (khirqa). Marries the daughter of another Basra Sufi, Abu Yaqub al-Aqta — a marriage that lasts until his death.

Goes to Baghdad to consult the great master Junayd of Baghdad, the most influential Sufi teacher of the age. Junayd prescribes for him 'silence and solitude' — a discipline Hallaj will repeatedly break.

Sets out on his first pilgrimage to Mecca, against Junayd's advice. Vows to remain for one year in the courtyard of the Sacred Mosque in continuous fasting and total silence. The retreat marks the beginning of his unconventional, public mystical career.

Returns from Mecca and astonishes his Sufi peers by removing the patched Sufi robe and adopting ordinary clothing — saying he wants to preach more freely to ordinary people. The Baghdad Sufi establishment is scandalised. He breaks formally with Amr al-Makki and his father-in-law.

Spends about five years preaching across eastern Iran — in the Arab garrison towns and frontier monasteries (ribats) of Khurasan, where volunteers gather to fight on the borders of Islam. He is given the nickname Hallaj al-Asrar, 'the Carder of Hearts', because he speaks people's secrets back to them.

Returns to Iraq, settles his family in Baghdad, and makes his second pilgrimage to Mecca, this time with four hundred disciples following him. Some of his old Sufi friends in Mecca accuse him publicly of sorcery and of making a pact with the jinn.

Sets out on a long voyage by sea to 'the lands of polytheism, to call men to God'. Reaches Sindh in India, then travels overland through Khurasan into Transoxiana, and reportedly as far as the borders of Turkestan and Chinese Turkestan, preaching against dualism to the Manichaean Uyghurs. Letters later come back to him from these regions; the Indians call him Abu al-Mughith, the Khurasanians call him Abu al-Mihr.

Returns from his great eastern journey for his third and final pilgrimage to Mecca, dressed in an Indian loincloth and a patched cloak. There he prays to God to be made despised and rejected, 'so that God alone might grant grace to Himself through His servant's lips'.

Returns to his family in Baghdad. Begins his open public preaching, which now turns shocking: he proclaims his burning love of God and asks people to kill him, saying 'O Muslims, save me from God! He has made my blood lawful to you.' It is in this period that he is reported to have uttered the famous shath, 'Ana al-Haqq' — I am the Truth.

An attempt by Sunni reformers to depose the underage Caliph al-Muqtadir fails. The Shi'i vizier launches anti-Hanbali repressions that touch Hallaj's circle. Hallaj flees Baghdad and goes into hiding for three years.

Arrested in the city of Sus in Khuzistan and brought back in chains to Baghdad. Imprisoned in the caliph's palace prison, where he will remain for nine years. The conditions of his confinement vary with the rise and fall of factions at court — sometimes lenient, sometimes harsh.

Years of imprisonment in Baghdad. He continues to write and his disciples continue to visit him. He composes the Kitab al-Tawasin, a series of dense mystical fragments including a defence of Iblis as the truest lover of God. His son Hamd is with him in his last days and later writes the eyewitness account of his execution.

Condemned to death by a vizierial court on charges including the blasphemy of 'I am the Truth' and the heresy of recommending that pilgrims build local replicas of the Kaaba. On 26 March 922 (24 Dhu al-Qa'da 309 AH) he is brought out to the gibbet at Bab al-Taq in Baghdad. Flogged with three hundred lashes, then crucified. He kisses the wood of the gallows. His last recorded words ask forgiveness for his executioners. His body is burned and the ashes scattered into the Tigris.
